questions.explanation: Correct: prompt injection reaches the model because user input joins the prompt, so defence is layered: even a tricked agent executes only whitelisted skills, run_python has no os/filesystem/network access, and 8 steps bound how much a hijacked loop can do. Network security tools and auth mechanisms don't address in-prompt manipulation, and 'encrypting' a prompt the model must read is incoherent.

questions.question_text: Map the project's memory systems to the human-memory analogy used in the source.
options: Context window=working memory, project Q&A cache=episodic, RAG knowledge base=semantic, agent loop history=per-task working notes
Context window=procedural, cache=working, RAG=episodic
All four are semantic memory
RAG=working memory because it's fastest
questions.explanation: Correct: the last 6 conversation turns are working memory (session-only), the permanent Q&A cache is episodic (specific past events), ingested documents are semantic memory (facts/knowledge), and the ReAct history is the current task's scratchpad. Calling the context window 'procedural' or the cache 'working memory' shuffles the analogy, the four are explicitly DIFFERENT types, and RAG is one of the slower layers (~200-550ms), not the fastest.

questions.question_text: Order the memory hierarchy from fastest to slowest retrieval.
options: Web search → RAG → cache → context window
Context window (0ms) → project cache (~10ms) → RAG knowledge base (~200-550ms) → web search (2-30s)
RAG → cache → context → web
Cache → context → web → RAG
questions.explanation: Correct: content already in context costs nothing; the semantic cache answers in ~10ms; RAG retrieval takes ~200-550ms; web search takes seconds — which is exactly why the retrieval decision tree checks the fast layers first. The other orderings invert or scramble that speed gradient.

questions.question_text: Order the multimodal ingestion pipeline for a chart inside a PDF.
options: PyMuPDF extracts the image → Pillow resizes to 1024px → LLaVA writes a description → BGE embeds the description + CLIP embeds the image → LanceDB stores all fields
CLIP → LLaVA → PyMuPDF → Pillow → store
LLaVA extracts → BGE resizes → CLIP describes → store
Pillow extracts → PyMuPDF describes → store raw bytes only
questions.explanation: Correct: extract bytes (PyMuPDF) → resize (Pillow, LLaVA's optimal 1024px) → describe (LLaVA) → dual-embed (BGE on the description text, CLIP on the pixels) → store text, both vectors and raw bytes in LanceDB. The other orderings assign impossible roles — CLIP can't extract from PDFs, BGE doesn't resize images, and Pillow doesn't parse PDFs.

questions.question_text: In the retrieval decision tree, why does the agent consult the cache BEFORE project RAG, and RAG before the web?
options: The web is usually wrong
The cache is more accurate than RAG
Cheapest-first: cache is ~instant, RAG ~500ms, web 2-30s — escalate only when the cheaper source misses
Alphabetical order of module names
questions.explanation: Correct: a good researcher checks memory, then their notes, then the library/internet — the tree escalates by cost, stopping at the first sufficient source. Ordering isn't alphabetical, the web isn't presumed wrong (it's the freshest source, just slowest), and the cache isn't more ACCURATE than RAG — it's faster, and only valid when the same question was answered before.

questions.question_text: What is the 'virtuous cycle' in the project's web-search flow?
options: Old cache entries are recycled into training data
The agent searches the web twice for verification
Users rate answers, improving the model
Web results are INGESTED into the project RAG, so the next similar question answers locally and fast — knowledge compounds per research session
questions.explanation: Correct: search → ingest pages into the project store → next time it's already in RAG (faster, private) — every research session enriches the knowledge base. No user-rating loop or model training happens anywhere (the model never changes), and double-searching is not the described mechanism.

questions.question_text: When the agent searches MULTIPLE categories in one answer, which two stores does it combine?
options: The cache and the trash folder
Two random categories for diversity
Local RAG and a cloud replica
The user's selected category (curated library) plus the project's own accumulated knowledge (research notes)
questions.explanation: Correct: rag_targets collects the user's chosen category AND the project's own store — combining curated documents with knowledge accumulated from past web research. Categories aren't chosen at random (isolation exists precisely to prevent noise), and neither a trash folder nor any cloud replica exists in this local-first design.

questions.question_text: In the 7-layer retrieval quality stack, which layer pairing is CORRECT?
options: Layer 1 = embedding quality (all-MiniLM → BGE-large); Layer 6 = MMR diversity; Layer 7 = cross-encoder re-ranking
MMR is Layer 1 because diversity matters most
Layer 1 = cross-encoder re-ranking; Layer 7 = embedding quality
Layer 3 = hybrid search; Layer 5 = metadata richness
questions.explanation: Correct: the stack runs embedding quality → chunking → metadata richness → query expansion → hybrid search → MMR → cross-encoder re-rank (plus factual anchors as a bonus) — foundations first, precision refinements last. Putting cross-encoder re-ranking at layer 1 reverses the stack's ends, MMR isn't layer 1 (diversity refines an already-good pool), and metadata richness is layer 3 while hybrid search is layer 5 — not the other way round.

questions.question_text: Which sequence correctly describes the smart retriever's orchestration (Layer 2)?
options: Search once → return raw top-5
Re-rank → expand → merge → search
Anchor injection first, then everything else
Expand to 3 variants → hybrid query each → RRF merge → table routing → cross-encoder re-rank → anchor injection → top-5
questions.explanation: Correct: orchestration is who-calls-what-in-what-order — the retriever expands the query, searches each variant, fuses with RRF, applies table routing, re-ranks with the cross-encoder, injects factual anchors, and returns top-5. Re-ranking before searching is impossible, raw single-search skips the whole quality stack, and anchor injection is a LAST-step correction on an existing pool.

questions.question_text: How does the generator pattern give users a word-by-word streaming answer in the UI?
options: WebSockets bypass Python entirely
The full answer is computed, then split into words with delays
The UI polls the server every second
Each yield pushes an incremental UI update without blocking — the function emits states as the LLM produces tokens
questions.explanation: Correct: chat_respond() is a generator — yield history for the user message, for 'thinking...', then per token from the Ollama stream, then final sources; each yield updates the UI immediately. Polling and fake type-out delays are simulations of streaming (the second one adds latency), and the streaming logic is Python generators, not a WebSocket bypass.

questions.question_text: Cache saves run in a daemon background thread. What does the user experience if a save fails?
options: The app restarts the thread and blocks until saved
The answer disappears
An error dialog demands a retry
Nothing — the answer already displayed instantly; the failure is logged and never impacts the user
questions.explanation: Correct: threading.Thread(target=save_to_cache, daemon=True).start() decouples the save from the response — the user sees the answer immediately, and a failed save is logged (never silent) but non-blocking. Error dialogs and blocking retries would defeat the pattern's purpose, and the displayed answer is independent of cache persistence.

questions.question_text: What question does 'observability' answer about a system, per the car-dashboard analogy?
options: Can you see inside it while it runs — without opening the hood?
Is the code object-oriented?
How fast can it go?
How much did it cost?
questions.explanation: Correct: observability = can you see inside the system? The dashboard (speedometer, fuel, temperature) tells you how the car performs without opening the hood — hence live hardware bars, processing metrics with ETA, streamed agent reasoning, and structured logs. Speed, cost and code style are properties you might OBSERVE, not the definition.

questions.question_text: How do hardware metrics flow from sampling to screen?
options: The GPU driver draws an overlay
Metrics are written to a CSV and imported hourly
The UI queries the GPU directly on every frame
A background thread samples every 1s into a shared dict; a UI timer reads that dict every 2s and renders the bars
questions.explanation: Correct: producer/consumer decoupling — the sampler thread (pynvml/psutil, 1s cadence) updates a shared dict; the UI timer (2s cadence) reads and displays it, so slow rendering never delays sampling. Direct per-frame GPU queries would couple UI to hardware calls, and CSV batching or driver overlays aren't the described design.

questions.question_text: Why stream every THOUGHT/ACTION/OBSERVATION to the user live instead of showing only the final answer?
options: It saves tokens
It slows the agent down for safety
Transparency — the user sees exactly what the agent is doing and why at each step, building trust and enabling early correction
Regulators require reasoning logs
questions.explanation: Correct: agent transparency means the reasoning is observable in real time — you watch '💭 Thought 1: I need current NIFTY data' as it happens, so odd behaviour is visible immediately rather than after a wrong answer. It adds no artificial slowdown, no regulation is cited, and streaming displays MORE text, not fewer tokens.

questions.question_text: Why choose the logging module over print() statements?
options: print() can't output strings with variables
logging is faster than print
print() is deprecated in Python 3
Timestamps, severity levels (DEBUG/INFO/WARNING/ERROR), level filtering, and redirection to files/monitoring — consistent across modules
questions.explanation: Correct: structured logging gives every message a timestamp and level, lets production run at INFO while debugging runs at DEBUG, and can redirect to files or monitoring systems — none of which print() offers. print() is not deprecated, speed is not the issue, and print() formats variables fine; it's the missing structure that matters.

questions.question_text: Which question type does GraphRAG handle that flat chunk-based RAG fundamentally struggles with?
options: Multi-hop and global questions ('summarise the main themes across this corpus') via an LLM-extracted entity graph with community detection
Translating documents
Looking up a single exact number
Spelling correction
questions.explanation: Correct: Microsoft's GraphRAG builds a knowledge graph of entities/relations and detects communities, so it can traverse connections (multi-hop) and aggregate global context — flat top-k chunk retrieval only sees isolated passages. Exact-number lookup is classic hybrid/keyword territory, and spelling or translation aren't retrieval problems.

questions.question_text: What makes RAG 'agentic'?
options: Using a bigger embedding model
Wrapping retrieval in a reasoning loop — the agent judges sufficiency and relevance, reformulates queries, and searches again before answering
Running retrieval on a GPU
Caching all answers
questions.explanation: Correct: agentic RAG replaces the single retrieve-then-generate pass with a loop: 'Do I have enough? Is it relevant? Should I reformulate?' — the 2026 default for hard queries, and the natural evolution of the source project's decision tree. Model size, caching and hardware don't change the retrieval CONTROL FLOW, which is what 'agentic' refers to.

questions.question_text: In Anthropic's Contextual Retrieval, what gets prepended to each chunk before embedding?
options: The full text of the previous chunk
A short LLM-generated situating sentence explaining where the chunk fits in its document
A list of stopwords
A random UUID for tracking
questions.explanation: Correct: an LLM writes a brief context line ('This chunk is from the SLA section of X's Q4 report...') for every chunk, so the embedded text carries document-level context — sharply cutting failed retrievals. UUIDs add no semantics, duplicating the previous chunk is plain overlap (the source project's 128-word technique), and stopwords add noise, not context.

questions.question_text: How does semantic chunking decide where to split, versus the source project's 512-word splits?
options: It asks the user to mark boundaries
It splits at exactly 100 tokens
It splits where embedding similarity between adjacent sentences drops — topic shifts — rather than at fixed word counts
It always splits at page boundaries
questions.explanation: Correct: semantic chunking embeds sentences and cuts where similarity falls (a topic change), so chunks follow meaning instead of arbitrary counts; 2026 guidance is to start with recursive fixed-size splits and upgrade only when retrieval metrics justify the extra compute. Page boundaries and fixed tiny windows are unrelated heuristics, and manual marking defeats automation.

questions.question_text: What does LATE chunking do differently from chunk-then-embed?
options: It embeds the WHOLE document with a long-context model first, then splits the token embeddings — each chunk vector retains full-document context
It chunks after the user's query arrives
It skips chunking entirely
It only chunks the last page
questions.explanation: Correct: 'late' means splitting AFTER embedding — token embeddings computed in full-document context get pooled into chunk vectors, preserving long-range signals that independently-embedded chunks lose. It happens at ingest (not query time), chunks still exist (retrieval needs units), and it processes the whole document, not the tail.

questions.question_text: HyDE improves retrieval by embedding what, instead of the raw question?
options: A hypothetical LLM-written ANSWER to the question — answer-shaped text lands nearer real answer passages
The question translated to English
The question repeated three times
The document's table of contents
questions.explanation: Correct: HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) has the LLM draft a plausible answer, embeds that, and searches with it — because answers resemble answer passages more than questions do. Translation only matters cross-lingually, tables of contents aren't query surrogates, and repetition doesn't change an embedding's direction.

questions.question_text: Where does ColBERT's late-interaction sit in the bi-encoder ↔ cross-encoder trade-off?
options: Between them: per-token vectors compared via MaxSim at query time — near cross-encoder quality at medium speed
It replaces retrieval with generation
Identical to a bi-encoder
Slower and less accurate than both
questions.explanation: Correct: ColBERT stores a vector PER TOKEN and computes token-level MaxSim at query time — more interaction than one-vector bi-encoders, far cheaper than full joint attention (cross-encoders); ColBERTv2 is canonical, jina-reranker-v3's 'last but not late' is the 2026 twist. It dominates neither extreme but bridges them, and it's still retrieval/ranking, not generation.

questions.question_text: What does SPLADE (learned sparse retrieval) produce for a document?
options: A compressed summary paragraph
An image embedding
A dense 1024-dim vector
A weighted set of vocabulary terms — including relevant terms NOT literally present — servable from a standard inverted index
questions.explanation: Correct: SPLADE outputs sparse vocabulary-space vectors: learned term weights with expansions (a doc about 'GIL' also lights up 'lock', 'threading'), combining BM25's exact-match precision with learned synonymy on inverted-index infrastructure (pgvector 0.9 added sparse support). Dense vectors are the bi-encoder path, and images/summaries aren't its output.

questions.question_text: With Matryoshka embeddings, what happens if you keep only the first 256 of 1024 dimensions?
options: You retain a valid smaller embedding, typically losing only ~1-3% recall while slashing storage and search cost
Recall improves
The vector becomes unusable
Only exact-match search still works
questions.explanation: Correct: MRL training packs the most important information into the leading dimensions, so truncation yields a usable embedding at a small recall cost — supported by most 2026 models (OpenAI text-3, Gemini Embedding 2, Nomic, Jina v5). Ordinary embeddings WOULD break under truncation (that's the innovation), recall doesn't improve from losing information, and semantic search still works — that's the point.

questions.question_text: Binary-quantizing stored embeddings gives what trade-off?
options: Lossless compression
4-32x memory reduction for roughly 1-5% recall loss
No memory savings but faster CPUs
2x memory savings, 50% recall loss
questions.explanation: Correct: int8/binary vector storage cuts memory 4-32x at ~1-5% recall cost (Cohere Embed-3 ships native modes; mxbai-embed-large performed best in cited production evals) — the same precision-for-size trade as LLM weight quantization, applied to the vector store. The other options overstate the loss, understate the savings, or claim losslessness that 1-bit storage can't offer.

questions.question_text: What is MTEB, and why does it matter when choosing an embedding model?
options: The Massive Text Embedding Benchmark — the standard leaderboard comparing embedding models across tasks; 2026 retrieval leaders include NV-Embed-v2, Gemini Embedding and BGE-M3
A GPU stress test
A vector database benchmark for insert speed
An LLM chat quality arena
questions.explanation: Correct: MTEB scores embedding models across retrieval, clustering, classification and more — the reference for statements like 'BGE-large was the best open model' (true at the source's writing; superseded by 2026). It benchmarks EMBEDDINGS, not database inserts, GPUs, or chat quality (that's LMSYS-style arenas).

questions.question_text: What does BGE-M3 provide that BGE-large-en-v1.5 (used in the source project) does not?
options: Only a smaller model size
Image embeddings
Dense, sparse AND multi-vector (ColBERT-style) outputs from one model, multilingual, 8K-token inputs — hybrid search from a single model
A proprietary licence
questions.explanation: Correct: the M3 = multi-functionality, multi-linguality, multi-granularity — one model emits all three representation types (vs BGE-large's English-only dense vectors capped at 512 tokens), enabling hybrid retrieval without separate systems. It's not merely smaller, doesn't embed images (CLIP territory), and remains open.

questions.question_text: What does the KV cache store, and why do long chats eat GPU memory?
options: The user's chat history on disk
Copies of the model weights per user
Quantization tables
Each generated token's attention Keys and Values, so they aren't recomputed per new token — the cache grows with context length
questions.explanation: Correct: without the KV cache, every new token would recompute attention over the whole context; caching K/V per token makes generation incremental but consumes VRAM proportional to context length — why long conversations are memory-hungry and why 2026 serving work targets KV management. Weights aren't duplicated per user, chat-history persistence is an app concern, and quantization tables are unrelated.

questions.question_text: PagedAttention borrows which operating-system idea, to fix what problem?
options: Disk defragmentation, to fix model loading
Virtual-memory paging — KV cache in fixed-size mapped pages — cutting the traditional 60-80% KV memory waste to near zero
File permissions, to fix security
Process scheduling, to fix slow tokenization
questions.explanation: Correct: vLLM's PagedAttention allocates KV cache in fixed-size blocks mapped by a page table (like OS virtual memory), eliminating the fragmentation that wasted 60-80% of reserved KV memory — serving 2-3x more concurrent requests per GPU. Scheduling, permissions and defragmentation are different OS ideas solving different problems.

questions.question_text: How does FlashAttention speed up exact attention without approximating it?
options: It caches answers between users
IO-awareness: it tiles the computation to avoid writing the large attention matrix to slow GPU memory
It skips half the attention heads
It quantizes attention to 1 bit
questions.explanation: Correct: FlashAttention reorganises the computation into tiles that stay in fast on-chip SRAM, never materialising the full attention matrix in slow HBM — exact results, large speed/memory wins; successors like FlashInfer add block-sparse KV and JIT compilation. Skipping heads or 1-bit quantization would change results (approximation), and cross-user caching is a serving feature, not a kernel.

questions.question_text: In speculative decoding, what are the roles of the draft and target models?
options: The draft model answers easy questions, the target hard ones
Two models vote on each token
A small draft model proposes several tokens; the large target model verifies them in one parallel pass, accepting correct ones several-at-a-time
The draft model compresses the prompt for the target
questions.explanation: Correct: the small model cheaply guesses ahead; the big model checks the whole guess in a single pass — accepted tokens emerge in bursts, and output quality is unchanged because the target model has the final say. There's no question routing, prompt compression, or voting — verification, not delegation, is the mechanism.

questions.question_text: What does continuous batching fix compared to static batching in LLM serving?
options: It disables the KV cache
It compresses responses
It guarantees identical latency for all users
Finished sequences are evicted and new requests inserted mid-flight — no head-of-line blocking waiting for the slowest request
questions.explanation: Correct: static batches must wait until every sequence in the batch finishes; continuous batching swaps completed sequences out and new ones in at each step, keeping the GPU saturated (a vLLM cornerstone with PagedAttention). It doesn't compress output, can't equalise latency across different-length generations, and works WITH the KV cache, not against it.

questions.question_text: How can a Mixture-of-Experts model have huge total parameters but modest per-token compute?
options: It quantizes to 0 bits
It processes only every other token
A router activates only a few expert sub-networks per token — most parameters sit idle on any given token
Experts share all their weights
questions.explanation: Correct: MoE layers hold many parallel 'experts'; a learned router sends each token to a small subset (e.g., 2 of 8), so active compute per token is a fraction of total capacity (Mixtral, DeepSeek family). Zero-bit quantization is nothing, skipping tokens would corrupt text, and weight-sharing would collapse the benefit of having separate experts.

questions.question_text: How do native function-calling APIs improve on the source project's regex-parsed ReAct format?
options: They eliminate hallucinations
The model returns STRUCTURED tool calls against JSON-schema definitions — no fragile text parsing of THOUGHT/ACTION blocks
They make the model smarter overall
They remove the need for tool descriptions
questions.explanation: Correct: the API accepts tool definitions as JSON schema and returns structured calls, replacing 'temperature 0.1 + format rules + regex' fragility — the 2026 production norm. The base model isn't smarter, descriptions are still essential (the model still decides WHEN to call), and structured calling formats output without eliminating hallucination.

questions.question_text: How does constrained decoding GUARANTEE schema-valid JSON where prompting cannot?
options: It fine-tunes the model on JSON
It masks invalid tokens during sampling — tokens that would violate the grammar/schema can never be chosen
It retries until the output parses
It lowers temperature to exactly 0
questions.explanation: Correct: the decoder intersects sampling with a grammar — at each step, only tokens consistent with the JSON schema remain eligible, so invalid output is impossible by construction. Fine-tuning and temperature 0 raise compliance PROBABILITY without guaranteeing it, and retry loops are the workaround constrained decoding retires.

questions.question_text: What is MCP (Model Context Protocol), as of 2026?
options: A replacement that makes function calling obsolete
Anthropic's proprietary Claude-only plugin API

An open standard (Anthropic, Nov 2024; donated to the Linux Foundation's Agentic AI Foundation in Dec 2025) for how agents discover and call tools/data sources — supported by all major vendors, 10,000+ public servers
A GPU communication protocol
questions.explanation: Correct: MCP standardises tool/data integration for AI systems and went cross-industry — near-universal adoption with ~97M monthly SDK downloads by March 2026. It's open, not Claude-only; it has nothing to do with GPUs; and it WRAPS function calling in a discoverable protocol rather than replacing it — the underlying invocation mechanism is unchanged.

questions.question_text: Per 2026 guidance, when is LangGraph the right agent-framework choice over CrewAI?
options: When you have no Python experience
For stateful production workflows needing checkpointing, streaming, and human-in-the-loop durability — LangGraph's graph state machine leads enterprise deployment
When you want the fastest demo
LangGraph is never preferred
questions.explanation: Correct: the 2026 split: LangGraph for durable, stateful production (largest deployment footprint); CrewAI for fastest demo-to-prototype ergonomics (but weaker observability/error recovery); AutoGen/AG2 for research multi-agent patterns; first-party SDKs when you don't need heavy orchestration. 'Fastest demo' is CrewAI's win, and language experience isn't the differentiator.

questions.question_text: What is the sober 2026 guidance on multi-agent systems?
options: Agents must never communicate
Always use at least five agents
Specialised agents coordinating (with A2A protocols maturing alongside MCP) are powerful but add failure modes — prefer a single agent when it suffices
Multi-agent systems eliminate hallucinations
questions.explanation: Correct: planner/researcher/coder-style teams unlock parallel specialisation, and agent-to-agent coordination is standardising — but every added agent multiplies coordination failures, so single agents remain preferred where sufficient (top production shops still favour custom, minimal orchestration). Mandating five agents is cargo-culting, hallucination isn't cured by adding agents, and A2A exists precisely because agents DO communicate.

questions.question_text: What do LoRA adapters train, and what does QLoRA add?
options: Small low-rank adapter matrices beside frozen base weights (10-20x cheaper, ~90-95% of full quality); QLoRA runs that atop a 4-bit-quantized base for single-GPU training
The final layer only; QLoRA deletes layers
All weights at lower learning rate; QLoRA adds more epochs
Only the tokenizer; QLoRA adds vocabulary
questions.explanation: Correct: LoRA freezes the base model and learns small low-rank additions — the 2026 default starting point (with SFT); QLoRA quantizes the frozen base to 4-bit so large models fine-tune on one GPU (~80-90% of full quality, more runtime). The alternatives — full-weight training, tokenizer-only, or last-layer-only — describe different (or nonsensical) regimes.

questions.question_text: Why has DPO largely displaced the classic RLHF PPO pipeline (per the source's RLHF description) by 2026?
options: DPO only works on vision models
DPO needs no preference data at all
DPO optimises directly on ranked preference pairs — far simpler than training a reward model plus PPO — with competitive quality (~1-5K pairs); cousins include IPO, KTO, ORPO, GRPO
PPO was made illegal
questions.explanation: Correct: classic RLHF = SFT → reward model → PPO reinforcement loop; DPO collapses that into a direct loss over chosen/rejected pairs — simpler infrastructure, competitive results, hence the 2026 default. DPO absolutely still needs preference data (ranked pairs), no legal ban exists, and it's a text alignment method, not vision-specific.

questions.question_text: RAGAS-style evaluation scores a RAG system along which TWO axes?
options: Number of chunks and number of users
Retrieval quality (context relevance/precision/recall) and generation quality (faithfulness to context, answer correctness)
Model size and training cost
GPU speed and disk usage
questions.explanation: Correct: modern RAG evaluation separates 'did we fetch the right context?' from 'did the answer faithfully use it?' — RAGAS and DeepEval automate both, extending the source project's keyword-assertion suite with graded metrics. Hardware stats, corpus size and training cost measure the infrastructure, not answer quality.

questions.question_text: What is LLM-as-Judge, and what is its known weakness?
options: A human panel scoring models; too expensive
Using a strong LLM to grade another model's outputs against criteria — standard 2026 practice, but judge consistency/bias needs care (few-shot human anchors like FaithJudge help)
A regex that checks answer length
A model that writes legal opinions; too slow
questions.explanation: Correct: an LLM grades outputs for faithfulness/relevance at scale — competitive with specialised detectors — but judges can be inconsistent or biased, so few-shot human-annotated anchors improve agreement. It's not about legal opinions, it specifically REPLACES routine human panels, and it's semantic evaluation, not a length regex.

questions.question_text: What does a groundedness (faithfulness) check verify, and what should the system do when it fails with an abstention policy?
options: That the user is polite; warn them
That every claim in the answer is supported by the retrieved context; if unsupported, abstain — say 'I don't know' rather than answer anyway
Latency under 1s; retry faster
Grammar correctness; auto-fix typos
questions.explanation: Correct: groundedness ties each claim to retrieved evidence — the runtime cousin of the source's 'cite sources' rule; under an abstention policy, unsupported answers are withheld ('I don't know') instead of hallucinated, with guardrail filters wrapping input/output. Grammar, latency and politeness are entirely different checks.

questions.question_text: The source project used cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2. What are its 2026-era successors and their typical gain?
options: Only OpenAI embeddings
BM25, which replaced neural rerankers
BGE-Reranker-v2-m3 (open self-host), Cohere Rerank 4 (API), jina-reranker-v3 — typically +5-15 NDCG@10 over vector-only retrieval
No successors exist
questions.explanation: Correct: reranking advanced well past ms-marco-MiniLM — the 2026 menu spans open (BGE-Reranker-v2-m3, mxbai-rerank), API (Cohere Rerank 4), and novel designs (jina-reranker-v3), with cross-encoder rerankers typically adding +5-15 NDCG@10. Successors clearly exist, OpenAI embeddings are bi-encoders (different stage), and BM25 predates — not replaces — neural reranking.

questions.question_text: Why is pgvector called the 2026 default for most teams' RAG storage?
options: It requires no indexes
It's the fastest vector engine at any scale
Vector search inside the Postgres you already run — up to ~50M vectors, no separate system to operate; v0.9 added sparse vectors and speed-ups
It's the only free option
questions.explanation: Correct: the operational argument wins — teams already running Postgres get vectors, filtering, and transactions in one database, comfortably to ~50M vectors. It is NOT the fastest at every scale (dedicated engines win at billions), it definitely uses indexes (IVFFlat/HNSW), and free alternatives abound (Qdrant, LanceDB, Chroma).

questions.question_text: Per the 2026 landscape, when do Qdrant and Milvus each beat LanceDB (the source project's choice)?
options: Qdrant for production serving with fast filtered queries (single binary, payload index); Milvus for billion-scale Kubernetes-native deployments; LanceDB's niche is edge/desktop/embedded apps
Only on Windows
Only for image search
Never — LanceDB wins everywhere
questions.explanation: Correct: the 2026 consensus assigns niches — Qdrant as the lighter production default with integrated payload indexing, Milvus for billion-scale distributed clusters, and LanceDB exactly where the source project sits: local desktop/edge apps where running a server is overhead. LanceDB is excellent locally but not universally superior, and modality/OS aren't the deciding factors.

questions.question_text: How do IVF and Product Quantization (PQ) differ from HNSW as ANN strategies?
options: They are exact, HNSW is approximate
They only work on GPUs
IVF clusters vectors and searches only nearby cells; PQ compresses vectors into small codes — IVF-PQ trades some recall for far less memory than HNSW's graph at large scale
They index text, not vectors
questions.explanation: Correct: IVF partitions space into clusters (probe a few cells per query); PQ stores compact codes instead of full floats; combined they serve huge corpora in less memory than HNSW's neighbour graphs — all three are approximate. None is exact, GPU is optional, and they operate on vectors (the source's ANN entry lists IVF/FAISS/ScaNN as HNSW alternatives).

questions.question_text: What defines a 'reasoning model' (o3, Claude extended thinking, DeepSeek R1), versus ordinary chain-of-thought prompting?
options: Trained-in test-time compute: the model spends extra inference budget on private 'thinking tokens' before answering — much stronger on math/code/planning, slower and pricier
A larger vocabulary
They only run in the cloud
A rule engine bolted onto the LLM
questions.explanation: Correct: prompting a model to 'think step by step' (the source's CoT) elicits reasoning; reasoning models are TRAINED (largely via RL) to generate extended hidden reasoning and scale accuracy with thinking length — the 2026 market spans o3/o4-mini, Claude extended thinking, Gemini Deep Think, and open-weights DeepSeek R1/QwQ (which run locally). No rule engine or vocabulary change is involved.

questions.question_text: Given 1M-token context windows, when does RAG still beat stuffing everything into context?
options: Only for image documents
Never — long context replaced RAG
RAG always wins

When the corpus exceeds any window, freshness matters, costs must stay low, or citations are needed; long context wins for multi-step reasoning over bounded docs — 2026 practice is hybrid: retrieve broadly, reason long-context over results
questions.explanation: Correct: research shows long-context reasoning models outperform RAG on chained multi-step tasks over bounded material (strong reasoners sometimes see RAG UNDERPERFORM their baseline), while RAG remains essential for corpus scale, freshness, cost and citation — hence hybrid designs. Both absolutist answers ignore that trade-off, and modality isn't the dividing line.

questions.question_text: How does provider-side PROMPT caching differ from the source project's semantic cache?
options: Prompt caching stores user passwords
They are identical
Semantic caching happens on the GPU
Prompt caching reuses the processed PREFIX (system prompt, tool definitions, documents) to skip recomputation on later calls; the semantic cache reuses complete ANSWERS for similar questions
questions.explanation: Correct: they cache different layers — prompt caching saves the compute of re-reading an identical prompt prefix (big cost/latency savings on repeated system prompts and tools), while the semantic cache skips generation entirely by returning a stored answer when a question is ~similar (cosine ≥0.92). They complement each other; passwords are nowhere involved, and cache location isn't the distinction.

questions.question_text: How does free-threaded Python (PEP 703, 3.13+) change the source project's 'threads for I/O, processes for CPU' rule?
options: In no-GIL builds, threads run Python bytecode genuinely in parallel, so CPU-bound work can use threads too — the classic rule weakens as adoption grows
It makes threads slower
It removes threading from Python
It only affects Windows
questions.explanation: Correct: the GIL was WHY CPU-bound Python needed multiprocessing (separate interpreters); the optional free-threaded build removes that lock, letting threads use multiple cores for CPU work — shrinking multiprocessing's territory as libraries adapt. Threads don't get slower for parallel workloads, threading isn't removed (it's finally fully useful), and the change is cross-platform.
